title: Non-Abelian Mixer for QAOA on Hybrid Oscillator-Qubit Quantum Processors
authors: Thinh Le, Hansika Weerasena, Jianqing Liu
abstract: The realization of universal control in hybrid oscillator-qubit quantum processors enables the systematic design and implementation of quantum algorithms. However, the algorithmic development for such platforms remains at an early stage. While the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) has been extensively studied in both continuous-variable (CV) and discrete-variable (DV) quantum systems, its development in the hybrid CV-DV setting remains limited. In this paper, we propose a hardware-native non-Abelian mixer for QAOA on hybrid CV-DV quantum processors and develop a corresponding hybrid ansatz for the Max-Cut problem. We evaluate the proposed ansatz on unweighted Erdős-Rényi graphs and benchmark it against the standard transverse-field mixer using the approximation ratio and optimal-solution probability. Across all graph sizes and Fock cutoffs in our simulations, the proposed non-Abelian mixer consistently improves both expected solution quality and the probability of sampling an optimal solution relative to the transverse-field mixer. These results indicate that the proposed non-Abelian mixer is a promising building block for QAOA on hybrid oscillator-qubit platforms.
summary: This paper introduces a novel, hardware-native non-Abelian mixer designed for running QAOA on hybrid oscillator-qubit quantum processors. By implementing this mixer, the authors significantly improve the performance metrics—including solution quality and optimal sampling probability—for solving the Max-Cut problem compared to standard mixers. This work suggests a general principle: algorithmic design should leverage the specific, non-Abelian control primitives available on the target quantum hardware.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for advanced quantum algorithms, specifically QAOA, tailored for the unique architecture of hybrid continuous-variable (CV) and discrete-variable (DV) quantum processors.
main_result: The proposed hardware-native non-Abelian mixer consistently outperforms the standard transverse-field mixer, leading to measurable improvements in both the expected solution quality and the probability of finding an optimal solution for the Max-Cut problem.
method: The authors developed a hybrid CV-DV QAOA ansatz utilizing a non-Abelian mixer, which was then rigorously benchmarked against the standard mixer using simulation techniques.
model_or_system: The system models a hybrid quantum processor coupling bosonic oscillators (encoded via GKP states) to discrete qubits. The problem solved is the Max-Cut problem, governed by a specific cost Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Approximation ratio and the probability of sampling an optimal solution ($P_{opt}$).
important_parameters: Graph size ($N=4, N=5$), mixer depth ($d$), and the GKP envelope parameter ($\Delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the feasibility of implementing the non-Abelian mixer using native hardware primitives like conditional displacements and single-qubit rotations.
figures_summary: Figures compare the performance improvement of the NA-QSP mixer versus the standard mixer across different graph sizes and show the dependence of performance on mixer depth and the GKP parameter $\Delta$.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the gap in QAOA development for hybrid CV-DV systems. It then proposes the non-Abelian mixer and the corresponding ansatz. Finally, it evaluates this ansatz by benchmarking its performance against existing mixers using quantitative metrics.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, hardware-aware algorithmic improvement for variational quantum algorithms (QAOA) on emerging hybrid quantum hardware, which is highly relevant for researchers working on quantum control and quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Schematic of a hybrid CV-DV quantum processor, illustrated with superconducting microwave resonators locally coupled to superconducting qubits and neighboring resonators coupled by beam splitters.

Fig. 2: Hybrid CV-DV QAOA circuit for the proposed ansatz. In each layer k, Ex(√π/2, ∆) couples the GKP-encoded oscillator modes to the ancilla register, the Max-Cut cost unitary UC(γk) applies graph-dependent phases, and E† x(√π/2, ∆) transfers these phases back to the GKP encoding. The local mixers U (i) M (Θk) are then applied across oscillator–qubit pairs. A final Ex(√π/2, ∆) is applied before qubit readout. Here, mi and qi denotes the i-th oscillator mode and ancilla qubit, respectively.

Fig. 3: Numerical results for the proposed non-Abelian mixer: (a) and (b) show the improvement of the proposed mixer over the standard transverse-field mixer across 20 random graph instances, measured in approximation ratio and optimal-solution probability, respectively. (c) shows the mean performance over repeated optimizations as a function of mixer depth d with N = 4 graph instance and ∆= 0.45. (d) shows the corresponding mean performance as a function of ∆at fixed N = 4 and Nmax = 10. In (c) and (d), bars denote optimal-solution probability and lines denote approximation ratio.

Fig. 3a and 3b compare the proposed NA-QSP mixer with the standard transverse-field mixer by reporting the improvement in each metric. Here, improvement refers to the difference between the corresponding metric obtained with the NA-QSP mixer and that obtained with the transverse- field mixer. The most important observation is that the NA- QSP mixer yields a consistently positive improvement in both approximation ratio and optimal-solution probability across all
